title: Conversion Rate
when_to_use: measuring what percentage of website sessions result in a purchase
comparing conversion performance across traffic sources or channels
evaluating the impact of a site change or A/B test on purchase behavior
benchmarking against industry conversion standards
diagnosing why revenue changed — was it traffic volume or conversion efficiency?
business_definition: Number of sessions that resulted in at least one completed order divided by total sessions in the period, expressed as a percentage. A session is a contiguous period of user activity on the site (typically defined by a 30-minute inactivity timeout). Excludes bot traffic, internal test sessions, and orders with status cancelled, test, or fraud.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The efficiency of turning website visitors into buyers. Answers: of every 100 people who visit, how many actually purchase?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Revenue per session or order value. A 5% conversion rate with $10 AOV is very different from 5% with $500 AOV. Use ec_aov or revenue per session for monetization efficiency. Also does not measure micro-conversions (add-to-cart, email signup) — use ec_checkout_funnel for intermediate steps.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Conversion rate is heavily dependent on how 'session' is defined. A 30-minute timeout produces more sessions (and lower CVR) than a 24-hour window. Teams comparing CVR across tools (GA4 vs Mixpanel vs warehouse) often see different numbers because of different session definitions, not actual performance differences.

template:
WITH session_orders AS (
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.session_date,
    CASE WHEN COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS has_order
  FROM sessions s
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id
    AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
    AND s.session_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY s.session_id, s.session_date
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS total_sessions,
  SUM(has_order) AS converting_sessions,
  ROUND(SUM(has_order) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0), 2) AS conversion_rate_pct
FROM session_orders

inflation_causes.cause: Bot traffic excluded from sessions but bot-placed orders included
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If bot-detection filters remove bot sessions from the denominator but the corresponding bot-generated orders remain in the numerator (e.g., scraper bots that trigger checkout events), the conversion rate rises because the denominator shrinks while the numerator stays the same or grows.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.session_id, s.is_bot, s.user_agent, o.order_id, o.order_date FROM orders o LEFT JOIN sessions s ON o.session_id = s.session_id WHERE s.session_id IS NULL OR s.is_bot = true ORDER BY o.order_date DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Ensure bot-detection logic is applied symmetrically: if a session is flagged as bot, all associated orders should also be excluded from conversion calculations. Join orders to sessions and inherit the is_bot flag.

inflation_causes.cause: Session definition window too long, merging multiple visits into one
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the session timeout is set to 24 hours instead of 30 minutes, a user who visits 5 times in a day and buys on the 5th visit counts as 1 session with 1 conversion — a 100% conversion rate for that user. With a 30-minute timeout, it would be 1 conversion out of 5 sessions (20%).
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT AVG(session_duration_seconds) AS avg_duration, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY session_duration_seconds) AS median_duration, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY session_duration_seconds) AS p95_duration, MAX(session_duration_seconds) AS max_duration FROM sessions WHERE is_bot = false AND session_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
inflation_causes.fix: Standardize session timeout to 30 minutes (industry standard). If using a custom sessionization, document the timeout and ensure it is consistent across all reporting tools.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting only sessions with product page views in the denominator
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the denominator excludes sessions that never reached a product page (homepage bounces, blog readers, support page visitors), the conversion rate is artificially high because low-intent traffic is excluded. This is 'product page conversion rate', not 'site conversion rate', but is often reported without the qualifier.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN has_product_view = true THEN 'with_product_view' ELSE 'no_product_view' END AS session_type, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions, SUM(CASE WHEN has_order = true THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS converting FROM (SELECT s.session_id, MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'product_view' THEN true ELSE false END) AS has_product_view, MAX(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN true ELSE false END) AS has_order FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY s.session_id) sub GROUP BY 1
inflation_causes.fix: Always use total site sessions in the denominator for the headline conversion rate. If you want product-page CVR, label it explicitly as 'product page conversion rate' and report both.

inflation_causes.cause: Multi-device purchase attributed to the final session only
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a customer browses on mobile (3 sessions) and buys on desktop (1 session), only the desktop session gets the conversion. The mobile sessions stay in the denominator as non-converting. But if session stitching merges these into fewer sessions, the denominator shrinks and CVR rises without any behavioral change.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT s.device_type, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS converting_sessions, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0), 2) AS cvr_pct FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY s.device_type ORDER BY sessions DESC
inflation_causes.fix: Report conversion rate by device type separately. If cross-device stitching is applied, document it and understand that it will mechanically increase CVR by reducing session count.

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If bot detection is incomplete, automated crawlers, scrapers, and monitoring bots add thousands of non-converting sessions to the denominator. A site with 10% real sessions and 90% bot traffic would show ~10x lower conversion rate than reality.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', session_date) AS day, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN is_bot = true THEN session_id END) AS bot_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN is_bot = false THEN session_id END) AS human_sessions, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN is_bot = true THEN session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0), 2) AS bot_pct FROM sessions GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 14
deflation_causes.fix: Improve bot detection using user_agent patterns, IP reputation lists, and behavioral signals (zero page interaction time, no mouse events). Filter WHERE is_bot = false in all conversion queries.

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If the order table's session_id column is NULL for some orders (e.g., phone orders, API orders, offline POS), those orders do not join to any session and are excluded from the numerator. The denominator includes the sessions that led to those orders, but the conversion is not attributed.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN o.session_id IS NULL THEN 'no_session' ELSE 'has_session' END AS linkage, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1
deflation_causes.fix: Either link these orders to their originating sessions (if web-originated), or exclude non-web channels from both the session denominator and order numerator to keep the ratio internally consistent.

diagnostic_questions: Is the session definition consistent across the reporting tool and the warehouse? What is the inactivity timeout?
Are bot sessions being filtered from the denominator, and are bot-generated orders filtered from the numerator?
Did total session volume change, or did the number of converting sessions change — or both?
Is the conversion rate shift concentrated in a specific traffic source, device type, or landing page?
Were there any tracking outages (pixel failures, tag manager changes) that could cause sessions or orders to not be recorded?
Are offline or phone orders included in the order count but missing session attribution?

causal_relationships.then: more visitors are buying — likely a UX improvement, better product-market fit, or a successful promotion that increased purchase intent
conversion improved but traffic volume decreased proportionally — check if a paid channel was cut (fewer but higher-intent visitors remaining)
users are adding to cart but not completing checkout — the friction is in the checkout flow, not in product discovery or consideration

causal_relationships.if: conversion_rate down, session count up dramatically
causal_relationships.then: traffic quality degraded — a new traffic source is sending high-volume, low-intent visitors that dilute CVR. Check traffic source mix.

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Conversion rate drops >1pp WoW without traffic volume change
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_checkout_funnel shows payment step completion rate dropped
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A payment processing issue is blocking conversions at the final step. The conversion rate drops because users who intend to buy cannot complete payment. The checkout funnel isolates the problem to the payment step specifically. Root causes include payment gateway outages, expired SSL certificates, new fraud rules rejecting legitimate cards, or a broken payment UI after a deploy.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', e.event_timestamp) AS day, e.event_type, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions FROM events e WHERE e.event_type IN ('begin_checkout', 'add_payment_info', 'payment_submitted', 'payment_success', 'payment_failed', 'order_confirmed') AND e.event_timestamp >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '14 days' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Conversion rate increases after a site deploy
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_gmv does not increase proportionally — or total sessions dropped
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: The deploy may have broken analytics tracking, causing sessions to not be recorded for some visitors. Fewer tracked sessions (smaller denominator) with the same number of orders produces a higher CVR. This is a data quality issue, not a real performance improvement. Verify by checking raw session counts and pageview event volumes against the pre-deploy baseline.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('hour', session_date) AS hour, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN page_count > 0 THEN session_id END) AS sessions_with_pages FROM sessions WHERE is_bot = false AND session_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '3 days' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

anti_patterns.pattern: Using unique visitors instead of sessions as denominator
anti_patterns.why_wrong: A visitor who comes 5 times and buys once has a 100% visitor-level conversion rate but a 20% session-level conversion rate. Visitor-level CVR inflates the metric because returning visitors who eventually convert count as a single success regardless of how many non-converting visits preceded it.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.user_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.user_id), 0) AS cvr FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.user_id = o.customer_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS cvr FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false

anti_patterns.pattern: Dividing order count by session count without deduplicating multi-order sessions
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If a single session produces 2 orders (e.g., a customer completes one order and immediately starts another), the numerator counts 2 but the denominator counts 1, producing a CVR above 100% for that session. Conversion rate should be binary per session: did this session convert or not.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS cvr FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS cvr FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS conversion_rate_pct FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false AND s.session_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.session_date) AS week, s.traffic_source, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS converting_sessions, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0), 2) AS conversion_rate_pct FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, sessions DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH session_conversion AS (
  -- Grain: one row per session with binary conversion flag
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.session_date,
    s.traffic_source,
    s.device_type,
    CASE WHEN COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS converted
  FROM sessions s
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id
    AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY s.session_id, s.session_date, s.traffic_source, s.device_type
),
weekly_cvr AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('week', session_date) AS week,
    traffic_source,
    COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions,
    SUM(converted) AS conversions,
    ROUND(SUM(converted) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0), 2) AS cvr_pct
  FROM session_conversion
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
with_trend AS (
  SELECT
    week,
    traffic_source,
    sessions,
    conversions,
    cvr_pct,
    LAG(cvr_pct) OVER (PARTITION BY traffic_source ORDER BY week) AS prev_week_cvr,
    ROUND(cvr_pct - LAG(cvr_pct) OVER (PARTITION BY traffic_source ORDER BY week), 2) AS cvr_change_pp
  FROM weekly_cvr
)
SELECT
  week,
  traffic_source,
  sessions,
  conversions,
  cvr_pct,
  prev_week_cvr,
  cvr_change_pp
FROM with_trend
ORDER BY week DESC, sessions DESC

title: Cart Abandonment Rate
intent_tags: add to cart but no purchase
why are people leaving their carts
when_to_use: measuring what percentage of shopping carts are created but never completed
identifying friction points between add-to-cart and purchase
evaluating the effectiveness of cart recovery emails or retargeting
comparing abandonment rates across device types or traffic sources
estimating lost revenue from incomplete purchases
business_definition: Percentage of shopping cart sessions where at least one item was added to cart but no order was completed. Calculated as (carts created - carts that converted to orders) / carts created × 100. A cart is identified by cart_id or session_id with at least one add_to_cart event. Excludes bot sessions and test orders.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The proportion of demonstrated purchase intent (adding an item to cart) that fails to convert to a completed order. Answers: how much demand are we losing between consideration and purchase?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Why users abandon. The rate alone tells you how many, not why. Common reasons include unexpected shipping costs, account creation requirements, payment failures, and comparison shopping. Use ec_checkout_funnel step-level analysis and session replay tools to diagnose causes.
metric_nature.common_misconception: A 'good' cart abandonment rate is not 0%. Industry averages are 65-80%. Many users add to cart as a bookmarking or price-comparison mechanism with no immediate intent to purchase. Attempting to reduce abandonment to near-zero often means restricting what counts as a cart, not improving the checkout experience.

template:
WITH cart_sessions AS (
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.session_date,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_add_to_cart,
    MAX(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_order
  FROM sessions s
  JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id
    AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
    AND s.session_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY s.session_id, s.session_date
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = 1 THEN session_id END) AS cart_sessions,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = 1 AND has_order = 0 THEN session_id END) AS abandoned_sessions,
  ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = 1 AND has_order = 0 THEN session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = 1 THEN session_id END), 0), 2) AS abandonment_rate_pct
FROM cart_sessions

inflation_causes.cause: Wishlist or save-for-later actions counted as add-to-cart
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If 'save for later' or 'add to wishlist' events are logged with event_type = 'add_to_cart', users who are bookmarking products (with no purchase intent) are counted as cart creators. Since they rarely convert in the same session, the abandonment rate is inflated with false intent signals.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT e.event_type, e.event_properties, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN e.session_id END) AS converting_sessions FROM events e LEFT JOIN orders o ON e.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE e.event_type IN ('add_to_cart', 'add_to_wishlist', 'save_for_later') GROUP BY e.event_type, e.event_properties ORDER BY sessions DESC
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to event_type = 'add_to_cart' only and verify that wishlist events use a distinct event_type. Audit your event taxonomy for overlapping or ambiguous cart-related events.

inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: Price-monitoring bots and scraper bots may trigger add_to_cart events to check pricing or availability. These sessions never convert because the bot has no purchase intent, but they inflate the cart count in the denominator.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT s.user_agent, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN s.session_id END) AS cart_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS converting_sessions FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.session_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days' GROUP BY s.user_agent HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN s.session_id END) > 0 AND COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) = 0 ORDER BY cart_sessions DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Apply bot detection before computing abandonment. Filter WHERE s.is_bot = false. Investigate high-cart-zero-conversion user agents for bot patterns.

inflation_causes.cause: Cross-session cart persistence counting each session as a new cart
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a user adds to cart in Session A, leaves, returns in Session B and completes the purchase, Session A is counted as an abandoned cart and Session B as a converted cart. The same purchase intent is counted twice — once as abandoned and once as converted — inflating the abandonment rate.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT c.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions_with_cart, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS converting_sessions FROM sessions s JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id JOIN customers c ON s.user_id = c.customer_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' AND s.is_bot = false GROUP BY c.customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) > 1 ORDER BY sessions_with_cart DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Use cart_id (persistent across sessions) rather than session_id as the unit of analysis. A cart that was created in Session A and completed in Session B is one converted cart, not one abandoned and one converted.

deflation_causes.cause: Only counting carts that reach the checkout page
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If abandonment is calculated only from sessions that reached the checkout page (rather than all sessions with add_to_cart), users who added to cart but bounced before reaching checkout are excluded. This artificially lowers the abandonment rate by removing the most immediate dropoffs.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = true AND has_checkout = false THEN session_id END) AS cart_no_checkout, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = true AND has_checkout = true AND has_order = false THEN session_id END) AS checkout_abandoned, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_add_to_cart = true AND has_order = true THEN session_id END) AS converted FROM (SELECT s.session_id, MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN true ELSE false END) AS has_add_to_cart, MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'begin_checkout' THEN true ELSE false END) AS has_checkout, MAX(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN true ELSE false END) AS has_order FROM sessions s JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY s.session_id) sub
deflation_causes.fix: Use add_to_cart as the entry point for abandonment calculation, not begin_checkout. Cart abandonment should measure all lost demand from the moment intent is shown.

deflation_causes.cause: Successful cart recovery emails not counted as the original cart
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If a user abandons their cart, receives a recovery email, and completes the purchase via a new session, the original cart is still marked abandoned (no order in that session) even though the business ultimately captured the revenue. This overstates true demand loss.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', o.order_date) AS week, o.traffic_source, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND o.traffic_source IN ('cart_recovery_email', 'abandoned_cart_email', 'email_remarketing') GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1 DESC
deflation_causes.fix: Report both raw abandonment rate and net abandonment rate (adjusting for carts recovered through remarketing). Link recovered orders back to the original cart_id to close the loop.

diagnostic_questions: Is abandonment measured at the session level or the cart level (persistent across sessions)?
Are wishlist or save-for-later events being conflated with add-to-cart events?
Has bot traffic been filtered before calculating the abandonment rate?
Is the abandonment spike concentrated on a specific device type (especially mobile)?
Did shipping costs, tax calculations, or payment options change recently?
Are cart recovery emails driving conversions that are not being attributed back to the original abandoned cart?

causal_relationships.then: the same demand is being lost — users show purchase intent (add to cart) but fail to complete, directly reducing conversion
higher-value carts are being abandoned at a greater rate — likely sticker shock at checkout when shipping or taxes are added to an already large basket
mobile checkout UX friction — check for form usability issues, payment method availability, or page load speed on mobile devices
the conversion drop is upstream of add-to-cart — fewer sessions are reaching the cart stage. Check product discovery, search, and browsing experience.

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Cart abandonment spikes >10pp in a single week
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_checkout_funnel shows payment step conversion collapsing
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Payment processing failure or regression. Users add to cart normally, proceed through checkout, but cannot complete payment. This causes abandonment to spike (carts are created but not completed) and the checkout funnel's payment step to collapse simultaneously. The upstream cause is typically a payment gateway outage, a new fraud filter rejecting legitimate transactions, or a broken payment form after a code deploy.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('day', e.event_timestamp) AS day, e.event_type, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions FROM events e WHERE e.event_type IN ('add_to_cart', 'begin_checkout', 'add_payment_info', 'payment_submitted', 'payment_success', 'payment_failed') AND e.event_timestamp >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '14 days' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, e.event_type

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Cart abandonment increases gradually over several weeks
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_aov is increasing in the same period
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A pricing or merchandising change is driving higher cart values, which leads to more sticker shock at checkout. As AOV rises (larger baskets), the perceived cost at checkout increases, particularly when shipping fees are flat or tiered. Users add expensive items, see the total, and bail. Both metrics move because the same pricing dynamic inflates basket size while reducing completion willingness.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.session_date) AS week, AVG(c.cart_value) AS avg_cart_value, AVG(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN c.cart_value END) AS avg_converted_cart_value, AVG(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN c.cart_value END) AS avg_abandoned_cart_value FROM sessions s JOIN carts c ON s.session_id = c.session_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false AND c.cart_value > 0 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

anti_patterns.pattern: Using orders / sessions as an inverse proxy for abandonment
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Cart abandonment is NOT (1 - conversion_rate). Conversion rate uses total sessions as the denominator, but cart abandonment uses only sessions with add-to-cart as the denominator. If 100 people visit, 40 add to cart, and 10 buy, conversion rate is 10% but cart abandonment is 75%. Inverting CVR gives 90%, which is wrong.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT 100 - (COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0)) AS cart_abandonment_pct FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id WHERE s.is_bot = false
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH cart_sessions AS (
  SELECT s.session_id,
    MAX(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_order
  FROM sessions s
  JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id AND e.event_type = 'add_to_cart'
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud')
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY s.session_id
)
SELECT ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_order = 0 THEN session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0), 2) AS cart_abandonment_pct FROM cart_sessions

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting empty carts (zero items) as abandoned
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Some implementations create a cart_id on page load or session start, before any items are added. If these empty carts are included, the abandonment rate is inflated with 'carts' that never had any items — the user never showed purchase intent.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN c.cart_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT c.cart_id), 0) AS abandonment_rate FROM carts c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.cart_id = o.cart_id
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN c.cart_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT c.cart_id), 0) AS abandonment_rate FROM carts c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.cart_id = o.cart_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') WHERE c.item_count > 0

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN has_order = 0 THEN session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0) AS cart_abandonment_pct FROM (SELECT s.session_id, MAX(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_order FROM sessions s JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id AND e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false AND s.session_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY s.session_id) cart_sessions
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.session_date) AS week, s.device_type, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS cart_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS converted, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS abandoned, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0), 2) AS abandonment_rate_pct FROM sessions s JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id AND e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, cart_sessions DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH cart_sessions AS (
  -- Grain: one row per session that had at least one add_to_cart event
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.session_date,
    s.device_type,
    s.traffic_source,
    MAX(CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS converted,
    COUNT(DISTINCT e.product_id) AS products_carted
  FROM sessions s
  JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id
    AND e.event_type = 'add_to_cart'
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id
    AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY s.session_id, s.session_date, s.device_type, s.traffic_source
),
weekly_abandonment AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('week', session_date) AS week,
    device_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS cart_sessions,
    SUM(converted) AS converted_sessions,
    COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) - SUM(converted) AS abandoned_sessions,
    ROUND((COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) - SUM(converted)) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0), 2) AS abandonment_rate_pct,
    AVG(products_carted) AS avg_products_carted
  FROM cart_sessions
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
with_trend AS (
  SELECT
    week,
    device_type,
    cart_sessions,
    converted_sessions,
    abandoned_sessions,
    abandonment_rate_pct,
    avg_products_carted,
    LAG(abandonment_rate_pct) OVER (PARTITION BY device_type ORDER BY week) AS prev_week_rate,
    ROUND(abandonment_rate_pct - LAG(abandonment_rate_pct) OVER (PARTITION BY device_type ORDER BY week), 2) AS rate_change_pp
  FROM weekly_abandonment
)
SELECT * FROM with_trend
ORDER BY week DESC, cart_sessions DESC

title: Checkout Funnel Drop-off Rate
when_to_use: identifying which specific step in the checkout process loses the most users
comparing funnel performance before and after a checkout redesign
diagnosing why cart abandonment increased — which step broke?
measuring the impact of adding or removing checkout steps (guest checkout, account creation)
benchmarking checkout friction across device types
business_definition: Step-by-step conversion rates through the checkout process, measured as the percentage of sessions that progress from one step to the next. Standard steps are: add_to_cart → begin_checkout → add_shipping_info → add_payment_info → order_confirmed. Each step's rate is sessions reaching that step / sessions reaching the previous step. Excludes bot sessions.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: Where in the checkout flow users give up. Answers: is the problem at the shipping step, the payment step, or the account creation step?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Why users drop off at a specific step. The funnel shows which step loses users, but not the reason. A 40% drop at the shipping step could be unexpected costs, slow load time, or address validation errors. Pair with session replay, error logs, and user research for diagnosis.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Funnel steps are not always sequential. Users may skip steps (e.g., saved payment info skips add_payment_info), revisit previous steps, or trigger events out of order. A strict sequential funnel model will undercount users at later steps if they did not trigger every intermediate event.

template:
WITH funnel_events AS (
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_add_to_cart,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'begin_checkout' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_begin_checkout,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_shipping_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_shipping,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_payment_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_payment,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'order_confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_order_confirmed
  FROM sessions s
  JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
    AND s.session_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY s.session_id
)
SELECT
  SUM(step_add_to_cart) AS add_to_cart,
  SUM(step_begin_checkout) AS begin_checkout,
  SUM(step_shipping) AS add_shipping,
  SUM(step_payment) AS add_payment,
  SUM(step_order_confirmed) AS order_confirmed,
  ROUND(SUM(step_begin_checkout) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_add_to_cart), 0), 2) AS cart_to_checkout_pct,
  ROUND(SUM(step_shipping) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_begin_checkout), 0), 2) AS checkout_to_shipping_pct,
  ROUND(SUM(step_payment) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_shipping), 0), 2) AS shipping_to_payment_pct,
  ROUND(SUM(step_order_confirmed) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_payment), 0), 2) AS payment_to_order_pct
FROM funnel_events

inflation_causes.cause: Page reload or back-navigation firing duplicate step events
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a user clicks 'back' and re-submits their shipping info, the add_shipping_info event fires twice. Since the funnel uses MAX(CASE WHEN ...) per session, duplicates within a session do not inflate that session's count. However, if events are sessionized incorrectly and the reload creates a new session_id, the same user's checkout attempt is counted across multiple sessions, inflating the numerator at intermediate steps.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT e.session_id, e.event_type, COUNT(*) AS event_count FROM events e WHERE e.event_type IN ('add_to_cart', 'begin_checkout', 'add_shipping_info', 'add_payment_info', 'order_confirmed') GROUP BY e.session_id, e.event_type HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY event_count DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Use MAX() per session_id to collapse duplicates. Verify that session_id is stable through the checkout flow — no mid-checkout session breaks due to domain changes (e.g., redirecting to a payment processor on a different domain).

inflation_causes.cause: Non-sequential funnel counting users who skipped steps
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the funnel counts anyone who triggered step N regardless of whether they triggered step N-1, users who skip steps (returning customers with saved payment) appear at later steps without passing through earlier ones. This inflates later-step counts and makes intermediate step-to-step conversion rates exceed 100%.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN step_payment = 1 AND step_shipping = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS payment_without_shipping, SUM(CASE WHEN step_order_confirmed = 1 AND step_payment = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS order_without_payment, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS total_funnel_sessions FROM (SELECT s.session_id, MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_shipping_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_shipping, MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_payment_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_payment, MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'order_confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_order_confirmed FROM sessions s JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY s.session_id) sub
inflation_causes.fix: Decide whether your funnel is strict-sequential or unordered. For strict, only count a session at step N if it also completed step N-1. For unordered, accept that step-to-step rates may exceed 100% and document this behavior.

inflation_causes.cause: Server-side events mixed with client-side events creating phantom steps
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If some funnel events are fired client-side (begin_checkout via JavaScript) and others server-side (order_confirmed via backend), latency or failures in one system can create inconsistent step counts. A server-side order_confirmed without a client-side begin_checkout makes the final step appear to have more entries than the entry step.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT e.event_source, e.event_type, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions FROM events e WHERE e.event_type IN ('add_to_cart', 'begin_checkout', 'add_shipping_info', 'add_payment_info', 'order_confirmed') GROUP BY e.event_source, e.event_type ORDER BY e.event_type, e.event_source
inflation_causes.fix: Standardize event sourcing — either all client-side or reconcile server-side events with client-side session_ids. If mixing, document which events come from which source and account for gaps.

deflation_causes.cause: Ad blocker or tracking prevention blocking client-side funnel events
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If funnel events are tracked via JavaScript that ad blockers or Safari ITP suppress, sessions from users with ad blockers will show add_to_cart (if server-side) but missing begin_checkout, add_shipping_info, etc. These sessions appear to drop off at step 1 even though the user completed checkout. Completion rates at intermediate steps are understated.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT s.browser, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS total_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e_atc.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS with_add_to_cart, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e_bc.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS with_begin_checkout, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS with_order FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN events e_atc ON s.session_id = e_atc.session_id AND e_atc.event_type = 'add_to_cart' LEFT JOIN events e_bc ON s.session_id = e_bc.session_id AND e_bc.event_type = 'begin_checkout' LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY s.browser ORDER BY total_sessions DESC
deflation_causes.fix: Use server-side event tracking for critical funnel steps. If client-side only, measure the gap between server-confirmed orders and client-side order_confirmed events to estimate the ad-blocker blind spot.

deflation_causes.cause: Single-page checkout not firing intermediate step events
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If the checkout is a single-page accordion or wizard and intermediate step events (add_shipping_info, add_payment_info) are only fired on page navigation (not form section completion), all intermediate steps show 0 sessions. The funnel jumps from begin_checkout to order_confirmed with no data in between.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT e.event_type, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions FROM events e WHERE e.event_type IN ('add_to_cart', 'begin_checkout', 'add_shipping_info', 'add_payment_info', 'order_confirmed') AND e.event_timestamp >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days' GROUP BY e.event_type ORDER BY sessions DESC
deflation_causes.fix: Instrument each section of the single-page checkout to fire events when the user completes that section (form validation pass, section expand/collapse). Map these to the standard funnel step event names.

diagnostic_questions: Which specific step in the funnel has the largest absolute drop-off (not percentage — raw session count lost)?
Is the drop-off pattern different between mobile and desktop?
Did a recent code deploy change the checkout flow or add/remove a step?
Are all funnel events firing correctly — check raw event counts by type for the last 7 days?
Do returning customers with saved addresses/payment show different funnel shapes than first-time buyers?
Is there a correlation between funnel drop-off and page load time at the problematic step?

causal_relationships.if: shipping step drop-off increases, ec_cart_abandonment also increases
causal_relationships.then: users are seeing unexpected shipping costs or delivery estimates and abandoning — check if shipping rates or delivery time messaging changed
payment processing issue — gateway outage, new fraud rules, or expired payment processor credentials. Check payment error logs.

causal_relationships.if: begin_checkout drop-off increases but cart_to_checkout conversion is stable
causal_relationships.then: users are adding to cart but choosing not to begin checkout — likely using cart as a wishlist or comparison tool, not a purchase signal

causal_relationships.if: all step-to-step rates stable but overall funnel volume drops
causal_relationships.then: upstream traffic volume declined — the funnel efficiency is unchanged but fewer users are entering it. Check ec_conversion_rate and session counts.

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Payment step completion rate drops >15pp in a single day
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_conversion_rate drops sharply with session volume unchanged
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Payment gateway failure or regression. The funnel is healthy through shipping but collapses at payment, which directly causes the overall conversion rate to drop. Since sessions are unchanged (traffic is fine), the problem is isolated to the payment infrastructure. Check for gateway error codes, SSL certificate expiry, 3DS verification failures, or a code deploy that broke the payment form.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('hour', e.event_timestamp) AS hour, e.event_type, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'payment_failed' THEN e.session_id END) AS payment_failures FROM events e WHERE e.event_type IN ('add_payment_info', 'payment_submitted', 'payment_success', 'payment_failed', 'order_confirmed') AND e.event_timestamp >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '48 hours' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Shipping step drop-off increases gradually over 2-3 weeks
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_aov is rising in the same period
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Shipping cost increases or threshold changes. If free shipping was raised from $50 to $75, users with carts between $50-$75 now see a shipping charge they did not expect. Higher-AOV carts may include bulky items with higher shipping fees. The shipping step is where cost transparency hits, so it absorbs the drop-off. AOV rises because low-value carts (which would have had free shipping before) are the ones abandoning.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.session_date) AS week, CASE WHEN c.cart_value < 50 THEN 'under_50' WHEN c.cart_value < 75 THEN '50_to_75' WHEN c.cart_value < 100 THEN '75_to_100' ELSE 'over_100' END AS cart_value_bucket, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS cart_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e_ship.session_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS reached_shipping, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS converted FROM sessions s JOIN carts c ON s.session_id = c.session_id LEFT JOIN events e_ship ON s.session_id = e_ship.session_id AND e_ship.event_type = 'add_shipping_info' LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false AND c.item_count > 0 GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1 DESC, 2

anti_patterns.pattern: Requiring strict sequential ordering of funnel events
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Real user behavior is non-linear. Users go back to edit shipping, revisit product pages mid-checkout, or have saved payment info that skips steps. A strictly ordered funnel (step N only counts if step N-1 timestamp is earlier) will undercount users at later steps, making the funnel appear leakier than it is.
anti_patterns.bad_sql:
-- Strict ordering: only counts session at step N if step N-1 happened first
SELECT session_id FROM events WHERE event_type = 'add_payment_info' AND session_id IN (SELECT session_id FROM events WHERE event_type = 'add_shipping_info' AND event_timestamp < (SELECT MIN(event_timestamp) FROM events e2 WHERE e2.session_id = events.session_id AND e2.event_type = 'add_payment_info'))
anti_patterns.good_sql:
-- Unordered: did the session trigger each event at any point?
SELECT
  s.session_id,
  MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_cart,
  MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'begin_checkout' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_checkout,
  MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_shipping_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_shipping,
  MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_payment_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_payment,
  MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'order_confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_confirmed
FROM sessions s
JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id
WHERE s.is_bot = false
GROUP BY s.session_id

anti_patterns.pattern: Computing funnel percentages against the first step instead of the previous step
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If all percentages are relative to step 1 (add_to_cart), you see cumulative drop-off but cannot identify which step is the bottleneck. The shipping step might show 60% of add_to_cart and payment shows 55%, suggesting only a 5pp drop — but if begin_checkout was 65%, the real drop at shipping is 5pp and at payment is also 5pp. Step-over-step rates reveal where the biggest single-step losses occur.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT 'add_to_cart' AS step, 100.0 AS pct UNION ALL SELECT 'begin_checkout', SUM(step_checkout) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_cart), 0) UNION ALL SELECT 'shipping', SUM(step_shipping) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_cart), 0) UNION ALL SELECT 'payment', SUM(step_payment) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_cart), 0) FROM funnel_events
anti_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT
  SUM(step_cart) AS s1_cart,
  SUM(step_checkout) AS s2_checkout,
  ROUND(SUM(step_checkout) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_cart), 0), 2) AS s1_to_s2_pct,
  SUM(step_shipping) AS s3_shipping,
  ROUND(SUM(step_shipping) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_checkout), 0), 2) AS s2_to_s3_pct,
  SUM(step_payment) AS s4_payment,
  ROUND(SUM(step_payment) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_shipping), 0), 2) AS s3_to_s4_pct,
  SUM(step_confirmed) AS s5_confirmed,
  ROUND(SUM(step_confirmed) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_payment), 0), 2) AS s4_to_s5_pct
FROM funnel_events

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN sessions END) AS add_to_cart, MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'begin_checkout' THEN sessions END) AS begin_checkout, MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'add_shipping_info' THEN sessions END) AS add_shipping, MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'add_payment_info' THEN sessions END) AS add_payment, MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'order_confirmed' THEN sessions END) AS order_confirmed FROM (SELECT e.event_type, COUNT(DISTINCT e.session_id) AS sessions FROM events e JOIN sessions s ON e.session_id = s.session_id WHERE s.is_bot = false AND e.event_type IN ('add_to_cart', 'begin_checkout', 'add_shipping_info', 'add_payment_info', 'order_confirmed') AND s.session_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY e.event_type) step_counts
sql_assets.intermediate_sql:
WITH funnel AS (
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.device_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_cart,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'begin_checkout' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_checkout,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_shipping_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_shipping,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_payment_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_payment,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'order_confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_confirmed
  FROM sessions s
  JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY s.session_id, s.device_type
)
SELECT
  device_type,
  SUM(step_cart) AS add_to_cart,
  SUM(step_checkout) AS begin_checkout,
  ROUND(SUM(step_checkout) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_cart), 0), 2) AS cart_to_checkout_pct,
  SUM(step_shipping) AS add_shipping,
  ROUND(SUM(step_shipping) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_checkout), 0), 2) AS checkout_to_shipping_pct,
  SUM(step_payment) AS add_payment,
  ROUND(SUM(step_payment) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_shipping), 0), 2) AS shipping_to_payment_pct,
  SUM(step_confirmed) AS order_confirmed,
  ROUND(SUM(step_confirmed) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(step_payment), 0), 2) AS payment_to_order_pct
FROM funnel
GROUP BY device_type
ORDER BY add_to_cart DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH funnel_sessions AS (
  -- Grain: one row per session with binary flags for each funnel step
  SELECT
    s.session_id,
    s.session_date,
    s.device_type,
    s.traffic_source,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_cart,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'begin_checkout' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_checkout,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_shipping_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_shipping,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_payment_info' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_payment,
    MAX(CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'order_confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS step_confirmed
  FROM sessions s
  JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY s.session_id, s.session_date, s.device_type, s.traffic_source
),
weekly_funnel AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('week', session_date) AS week,
    SUM(step_cart) AS s1_cart,
    SUM(step_checkout) AS s2_checkout,
    SUM(step_shipping) AS s3_shipping,
    SUM(step_payment) AS s4_payment,
    SUM(step_confirmed) AS s5_confirmed
  FROM funnel_sessions
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_rates_and_trend AS (
  SELECT
    week,
    s1_cart,
    s2_checkout,
    ROUND(s2_checkout * 100.0 / NULLIF(s1_cart, 0), 2) AS cart_to_checkout_pct,
    s3_shipping,
    ROUND(s3_shipping * 100.0 / NULLIF(s2_checkout, 0), 2) AS checkout_to_shipping_pct,
    s4_payment,
    ROUND(s4_payment * 100.0 / NULLIF(s3_shipping, 0), 2) AS shipping_to_payment_pct,
    s5_confirmed,
    ROUND(s5_confirmed * 100.0 / NULLIF(s4_payment, 0), 2) AS payment_to_order_pct,
    ROUND(s5_confirmed * 100.0 / NULLIF(s1_cart, 0), 2) AS overall_funnel_pct,
    LAG(ROUND(s5_confirmed * 100.0 / NULLIF(s1_cart, 0), 2)) OVER (ORDER BY week) AS prev_week_overall_pct
  FROM weekly_funnel
)
SELECT
  *,
  ROUND(overall_funnel_pct - COALESCE(prev_week_overall_pct, 0), 2) AS overall_change_pp
FROM with_rates_and_trend
ORDER BY week DESC

title: Session-to-Purchase Rate
when_to_use: understanding how many sessions customers need before making a purchase
calibrating attribution windows to match actual consideration periods
comparing purchase decision speed across product categories or price points
evaluating whether site improvements are shortening the path to purchase
sizing the opportunity for remarketing based on multi-session buying patterns
business_definition: The number of distinct sessions a customer has before completing their first purchase, and the elapsed calendar time between first session and first order. Measured per customer, then aggregated as a distribution. Only includes customers who eventually purchased. Excludes bot sessions and cancelled/test/fraud orders.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The length of the customer consideration journey. Answers: is our product an impulse buy (1 session) or a researched purchase (5-10 sessions over weeks)?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Session quality or depth. A customer with 5 sessions of 2 seconds each (accidental clicks) is not the same as 5 sessions of 10 minutes each (research sessions). This metric counts sessions, not engagement depth. Pair with session duration and page depth for a fuller picture.
metric_nature.common_misconception: A lower sessions-to-purchase is not always better. For high-consideration products (electronics, furniture, B2B), multiple sessions indicate healthy research behavior. Forcing a single-session conversion on a $2000 product often means aggressive sales tactics rather than genuine purchase readiness.

template:
WITH customer_sessions AS (
  SELECT
    s.user_id AS customer_id,
    s.session_id,
    s.session_date,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY s.user_id ORDER BY s.session_date, s.session_id) AS session_number
  FROM sessions s
  WHERE s.is_bot = false
    AND s.session_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
),
first_orders AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order_date,
    MIN(o.session_id) AS first_order_session_id
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
)
SELECT
  AVG(cs_count.sessions_before_purchase) AS avg_sessions_to_purchase,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY cs_count.sessions_before_purchase) AS median_sessions,
  AVG(cs_count.days_to_purchase) AS avg_days_to_purchase
FROM (
  SELECT
    fo.customer_id,
    COUNT(DISTINCT cs.session_id) AS sessions_before_purchase,
    fo.first_order_date - MIN(cs.session_date) AS days_to_purchase
  FROM first_orders fo
  JOIN customer_sessions cs ON fo.customer_id = cs.customer_id
    AND cs.session_date <= fo.first_order_date
  GROUP BY fo.customer_id, fo.first_order_date
) cs_count

inflation_causes.cause: Bot sessions attributed to real user IDs
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If bot sessions are not filtered and share a user_id with a real customer (e.g., through cookie pollution or shared IPs), the customer's session count is inflated with visits they never made. A customer who visited 3 times appears to have visited 30 times if a bot inherited their tracking cookie.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT s.user_id, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS total_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.is_bot = true THEN s.session_id END) AS bot_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.is_bot = false THEN s.session_id END) AS human_sessions FROM sessions s WHERE s.user_id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY s.user_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.is_bot = true THEN s.session_id END) > 0 ORDER BY bot_sessions DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Apply bot filtering (WHERE is_bot = false) before computing sessions-to-purchase. Investigate user_ids with abnormally high session counts for bot contamination.

inflation_causes.cause: Session timeout too short, splitting real visits into multiple sessions
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the session timeout is 5 minutes instead of 30, a user who browses for 20 minutes with a 6-minute pause to read product reviews gets split into 2+ sessions. This inflates the sessions-per-purchase count without reflecting additional distinct visits.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT AVG(session_duration_seconds) AS avg_duration, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY session_duration_seconds) AS median_duration, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN session_duration_seconds < 60 THEN session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0) AS pct_under_1min FROM sessions WHERE is_bot = false AND session_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
inflation_causes.fix: Standardize session timeout to 30 minutes. If using a shorter timeout, document it and understand the mechanical effect on sessions-to-purchase metrics.

inflation_causes.cause: Cross-device sessions not stitched to a single user identity
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: A customer who researches on mobile (3 sessions as anonymous user) and buys on desktop (1 session as logged-in user) appears as 1 session-to-purchase on desktop. But if the mobile sessions are eventually linked via login, the count jumps to 4. Without stitching, the metric underreports for desktop buyers and is unavailable for anonymous mobile sessions. With stitching, the count may overreport if stale sessions from months ago are included.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT s.device_type, AVG(sub.session_count) AS avg_sessions_to_purchase FROM (SELECT fo.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT cs.session_id) AS session_count FROM (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) fo JOIN sessions cs ON fo.customer_id = cs.user_id AND cs.session_date <= fo.first_order_date AND cs.is_bot = false GROUP BY fo.customer_id) sub JOIN sessions s ON sub.customer_id = s.user_id GROUP BY s.device_type
inflation_causes.fix: Apply cross-device identity resolution before computing sessions-to-purchase. Include only sessions within a reasonable lookback window (e.g., 90 days before first purchase) to avoid including ancient unrelated sessions.

deflation_causes.cause: Anonymous sessions not linked to eventual purchaser
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If a user browses anonymously for 5 sessions and then logs in on session 6 and purchases, only session 6 has a user_id. Sessions 1-5 are anonymous and cannot be joined to the customer. The metric shows 1 session-to-purchase when the true count is 6.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS total_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.user_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS identified_sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.user_id IS NULL THEN s.session_id END) AS anonymous_sessions, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN s.user_id IS NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0), 2) AS anonymous_pct FROM sessions s WHERE s.is_bot = false AND s.session_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
deflation_causes.fix: Use device_id or cookie_id to link anonymous sessions to the user_id established on login. Backfill user_id on prior sessions that share the same device_id once a login event occurs.

deflation_causes.cause: First-touch session predates the data retention window
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If session data is retained for 90 days but a customer first visited 120 days before purchasing, their earliest sessions are gone. The metric shows fewer sessions-to-purchase than reality because the early consideration visits have been purged.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT MIN(session_date) AS earliest_session, MAX(session_date) AS latest_session, CURRENT_DATE - MIN(session_date) AS retention_days FROM sessions
deflation_causes.fix: Extend session data retention to at least match the longest typical consideration period for your product category. If retention is limited, document the truncation effect and acknowledge that sessions-to-purchase is a lower bound.

diagnostic_questions: Are anonymous sessions being linked to customer identities, or are only logged-in sessions counted?
What session timeout is being used — could it be artificially splitting or merging visits?
Is the sessions-to-purchase distribution showing a single mode (most buy in 1-2 sessions) or bimodal (some impulse, some research)?
Has the average price point of purchased products changed, which would affect consideration length?
Are cross-device sessions being stitched, and if so, what is the lookback window?
Is the metric including only first purchases, or all purchases (which would skew toward fewer sessions for repeat buyers)?

causal_relationships.then: customers are taking longer to decide, which means more sessions exist in the denominator without converting — this is natural and does not necessarily indicate a problem if total conversions are stable
the business may be shifting toward lower-priced impulse products that require less consideration — verify product category mix

causal_relationships.if: sessions_to_purchase increases for a specific category only
causal_relationships.then: that category may have introduced higher-priced items, more complex products, or reduced trust signals (fewer reviews, less product detail)

causal_relationships.if: sessions_to_purchase drops after a site change
causal_relationships.then: improved product information, better search, or streamlined UX reduced the need for multiple research visits — but verify this is not just a tracking change that merged sessions

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Average sessions-to-purchase increases from 3 to 5 over a quarter
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_cart_abandonment is also rising in the same period
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Increased checkout friction is forcing users to make multiple attempts before successfully completing a purchase. Users add to cart, hit a problem (payment error, shipping cost shock, account creation wall), abandon, return in a new session, and try again. The same friction event drives both metrics: longer paths to purchase and higher per-session abandonment. Check for new checkout steps, payment issues, or mandatory registration requirements added recently.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', fo.first_order_date) AS purchase_month, AVG(sub.session_count) AS avg_sessions_to_purchase, AVG(sub.cart_sessions) AS avg_sessions_with_cart FROM (SELECT fo.customer_id, fo.first_order_date, COUNT(DISTINCT cs.session_id) AS session_count, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'add_to_cart' THEN cs.session_id END) AS cart_sessions FROM (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) fo JOIN sessions cs ON fo.customer_id = cs.user_id AND cs.session_date <= fo.first_order_date AND cs.is_bot = false LEFT JOIN events e ON cs.session_id = e.session_id GROUP BY fo.customer_id, fo.first_order_date) sub JOIN (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) fo ON sub.customer_id = fo.customer_id GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Sessions-to-purchase drops significantly after a product page redesign
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_conversion_rate improves in the same period
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: The product page redesign improved information density, trust signals (reviews, guarantees), or visual presentation enough that users need fewer visits to reach purchase confidence. Both metrics improve because the same upstream cause — better product pages — reduces research friction (fewer sessions) and increases per-session conversion (higher CVR). Verify by checking product page engagement metrics (time on page, scroll depth) before and after the change.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', s.session_date) AS week, AVG(s.session_duration_seconds) AS avg_session_duration, AVG(s.page_count) AS avg_pages_per_session, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN e.event_type = 'product_view' THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS pct_sessions_with_product_view, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_id IS NOT NULL THEN s.session_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id), 0) AS cvr_pct FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN events e ON s.session_id = e.session_id LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.session_id = o.session_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

anti_patterns.pattern: Computing average sessions-to-purchase including non-purchasers as 0
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Users who never purchase should not be included in the sessions-to-purchase calculation. If you include 10,000 non-purchasers as 0 and 100 purchasers averaging 4 sessions, the mean becomes 0.04 instead of 4. The metric becomes a variant of conversion rate rather than a measure of purchase consideration length.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT s.user_id, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions, CASE WHEN COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS purchased FROM sessions s LEFT JOIN orders o ON s.user_id = o.customer_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') WHERE s.is_bot = false GROUP BY s.user_id
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH purchasers AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  p.customer_id,
  COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions_before_purchase
FROM purchasers p
JOIN sessions s ON p.customer_id = s.user_id
  AND s.session_date <= p.first_order_date
  AND s.is_bot = false
GROUP BY p.customer_id

anti_patterns.pattern: Using only the converting session instead of all pre-purchase sessions
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If you only count the session in which the order was placed, sessions-to-purchase is always 1 for every customer. This is a tautology, not a metric. The value of this metric comes from counting all sessions leading up to (and including) the purchase.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.session_id) AS sessions_to_purchase FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH first_purchase AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT fp.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions_to_purchase
FROM first_purchase fp
JOIN sessions s ON fp.customer_id = s.user_id
  AND s.session_date <= fp.first_order_date
  AND s.is_bot = false
GROUP BY fp.customer_id

sql_assets.basic_sql:
WITH first_purchase AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT AVG(session_count) AS avg_sessions_to_purchase FROM (
  SELECT fp.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS session_count
  FROM first_purchase fp
  JOIN sessions s ON fp.customer_id = s.user_id AND s.session_date <= fp.first_order_date AND s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY fp.customer_id
) sub
sql_assets.intermediate_sql:
WITH first_purchase AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
session_counts AS (
  SELECT
    fp.customer_id,
    fp.first_order_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions_before_purchase,
    fp.first_order_date - MIN(s.session_date) AS days_to_purchase
  FROM first_purchase fp
  JOIN sessions s ON fp.customer_id = s.user_id
    AND s.session_date <= fp.first_order_date
    AND s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY fp.customer_id, fp.first_order_date
)
SELECT
  sessions_before_purchase,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers,
  ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id)) OVER (), 0), 2) AS pct_of_total,
  AVG(days_to_purchase) AS avg_days
FROM session_counts
GROUP BY sessions_before_purchase
ORDER BY sessions_before_purchase
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH first_purchase AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer with their first order date
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
session_journey AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer with pre-purchase session stats
  SELECT
    fp.customer_id,
    fp.first_order_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_id) AS sessions_before_purchase,
    fp.first_order_date - MIN(s.session_date) AS days_to_purchase,
    SUM(s.session_duration_seconds) AS total_browsing_seconds,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.device_type) AS devices_used
  FROM first_purchase fp
  JOIN sessions s ON fp.customer_id = s.user_id
    AND s.session_date <= fp.first_order_date
    AND s.is_bot = false
  GROUP BY fp.customer_id, fp.first_order_date
),
cohort_journey AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', first_order_date) AS purchase_month,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS purchasers,
    AVG(sessions_before_purchase) AS avg_sessions,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY sessions_before_purchase) AS median_sessions,
    AVG(days_to_purchase) AS avg_days,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY days_to_purchase) AS median_days,
    AVG(total_browsing_seconds) / NULLIF(AVG(sessions_before_purchase), 0) AS avg_seconds_per_session
  FROM session_journey
  GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT
  purchase_month,
  purchasers,
  ROUND(avg_sessions, 1) AS avg_sessions,
  ROUND(median_sessions, 1) AS median_sessions,
  ROUND(avg_days, 1) AS avg_days,
  ROUND(median_days, 1) AS median_days,
  ROUND(avg_seconds_per_session) AS avg_sec_per_session,
  LAG(ROUND(avg_sessions, 1)) OVER (ORDER BY purchase_month) AS prev_month_avg_sessions,
  ROUND(avg_sessions - LAG(avg_sessions) OVER (ORDER BY purchase_month), 2) AS sessions_change
FROM cohort_journey
ORDER BY purchase_month DESC
